title: Nephrology — Renal Medicine USMLE
description: Acute kidney injury, CKD, glomerulonephritis, nephrotic syndrome, acid-base disorders, electrolyte imbalances, renal pharmacology.
subject: medicine

cards.front: AKI: prerenal vs intrinsic vs postrenal — FENa values
cards.back: Prerenal: FENa < 1%. Intrinsic (ATN): FENa > 2%. Postrenal: variable (often > 2%)

cards.front: Most common cause of intrinsic AKI
cards.back: Acute tubular necrosis (ATN) — ischemic or nephrotoxic (aminoglycosides, contrast, myoglobin)
Massive proteinuria (> 3.5 g/day), hypoalbuminemia, edema, hyperlipidemia, lipiduria (Maltese cross bodies on urine microscopy)

cards.front: Most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in adults
cards.back: Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS) in Blacks; membranous nephropathy in Whites

cards.front: Most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children
cards.back: Minimal change disease (MCD) — responds to steroids; no changes on light microscopy; effacement of podocyte foot processes on EM
Hematuria (RBC casts), proteinuria (< 3.5 g/day), hypertension, oliguria, azotemia
2–4 weeks after pharyngitis or 6 weeks after skin infection. ↓ C3, ↑ ASO titer. 'Lumpy bumpy' deposits on IF

cards.front: IgA nephropathy (Berger's disease): trigger and IF
cards.back: Synpharyngitic hematuria (onset hours after URI). IgA deposits in mesangium on IF. Most common GN worldwide

cards.front: Rapidly progressive GN (RPGN): crescent formation and causes
cards.back: Crescents = proliferating parietal cells + monocytes. Types: 1) Goodpasture (anti-GBM), 2) immune complex (SLE, post-strep), 3) pauci-immune (ANCA — Wegener/MPA)
Anti-GBM antibodies attack type IV collagen in lungs and kidneys → pulmonary hemorrhage + RPGN. Treatment: plasmapheresis + steroids + cyclophosphamide

cards.front: Polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD): gene and associations
cards.back: PKD1 (chromosome 16, 85%) or PKD2. Associations: berry aneurysms, mitral valve prolapse, liver cysts, diverticulosis
Polycythemia (↑ EPO), hypertension (↑ renin), hypercalcemia (↑ PTHrP), Stauffer syndrome (elevated LFTs without metastasis)
Most common renal tumor in children. Associated with WAGR syndrome (Wilms, Aniridia, Genitourinary anomalies, intellectual disability Retardation). WT1 gene
eGFR < 60 mL/min/1.73m² for ≥ 3 months OR markers of kidney damage for ≥ 3 months
Hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis (↓ NH4+ excretion), hyperphosphatemia, hypocalcemia, secondary hyperparathyroidism, anemia (↓ EPO), hypertension
CKD → ↓ phosphate excretion → hyperphosphatemia → binds Ca → hypocalcemia → ↑ PTH → osteitis fibrosa cystica
Microalbuminuria (30–300 mg/day urine albumin). Histology: Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules (nodular glomerulosclerosis)

cards.front: Metabolic acidosis with high anion gap: causes (MUDPILES)
cards.back: Methanol, Uremia, Diabetic ketoacidosis, Propylene glycol/Paraldehyde, Isoniazid/Iron, Lactic acidosis, Ethylene glycol, Salicylates

cards.front: Metabolic acidosis with normal anion gap: causes
cards.back: Hyperchloremic acidosis: diarrhea (loss of HCO3−), RTA, saline infusion, ureterosigmoidostomy

cards.front: Renal tubular acidosis type 1 (distal): defect and feature
cards.back: Defect in H+ secretion in collecting duct. Urine pH > 5.5 despite acidemia. Causes: Sjögren's, amphotericin B. Leads to nephrolithiasis (calcium phosphate/oxalate)
Defect in HCO3− reabsorption in proximal tubule. Urine pH < 5.5 (distal H+ secretion intact). Associated with Fanconi syndrome
Decreased aldosterone → hyperkalemia + normal anion gap metabolic acidosis. Seen in diabetic nephropathy, ACE inhibitors, heparin
Vomiting (HCl loss), diuretics (loop/thiazide), Bartter syndrome, Gitelman syndrome, Conn's syndrome, milk-alkali syndrome

cards.front: Respiratory acidosis vs alkalosis: compensation rules
cards.back: Resp acidosis: HCO3− increases 1 mEq/L per 10 mmHg ↑ CO2 (acute) or 3.5 per 10 (chronic). Resp alkalosis: HCO3− decreases 2 per 10 (acute) or 5 per 10 (chronic)
Peaked T waves → prolonged PR → wide QRS → sine wave → ventricular fibrillation
1. IV calcium gluconate (cardiac membrane stabilization). 2. Insulin + glucose (shifts K into cells). 3. Sodium bicarbonate. 4. Albuterol. 5. Kayexalate/patiromer/hemodialysis (removal)
Hyponatremia + low serum osmolality + urine osmolality > serum osmolality + urine Na > 20 mEq/L + euvolemia

cards.front: Central DI vs nephrogenic DI: desmopressin response
cards.back: Central DI: responds to desmopressin (ADH deficiency). Nephrogenic DI: no response (ADH resistance). Both: polyuria + dilute urine + hypernatremia

cards.front: Most common cause of nephrogenic DI
cards.back: Lithium toxicity (also demeclocycline, hypercalcemia, hypokalemia)
Block Na+/K+/2Cl− cotransporter (NKCC2) in thick ascending limb of loop of Henle. Cause loss of Na+, K+, Cl−, Mg2+, Ca2+

cards.front: Thiazide diuretics: mechanism and unique feature
cards.back: Block Na+/Cl− cotransporter in distal tubule. Cause hypocalciuria (↑ Ca2+ reabsorption) — used for calcium nephrolithiasis. Cause hypokalemia, hyponatremia, hyperglycemia, hyperuricemia, hyperlipidemia
Aldosterone receptor antagonist (K+-sparing diuretic). Used in heart failure, hypertension, ascites, primary hyperaldosteronism, PCOS. Side effect: gynecomastia
Calcium oxalate (80%). Radiopaque on X-ray. Causes: hypercalciuria, hyperoxaluria (Crohn's), hypocitraturia
Radiolucent (not seen on plain film, seen on CT). Low urine pH (< 5.5). Associated with gout, myeloproliferative disorders, Lesch-Nyhan
Urease-producing bacteria (Proteus, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas). Form large staghorn calculi. Alkaline urine

cards.front: Renal artery stenosis: causes and finding
cards.back: Atherosclerosis (older males) or fibromuscular dysplasia (young women). Renovascular hypertension; flank bruit; worsening renal function with ACE inhibitor
Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia + thrombocytopenia + AKI. Most common cause in children: Shiga toxin-producing E. coli O157:H7 (STEC). Do NOT give antibiotics (↑ toxin release)

cards.front: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP): triad vs pentad
cards.back: Classic pentad: microangiopathic hemolytic anemia + thrombocytopenia + fever + renal failure + neurological symptoms. Caused by ADAMTS13 deficiency

cards.front: What is the Cockcroft-Gault equation used for?
cards.back: Estimates creatinine clearance (CrCl) to adjust drug dosing. CrCl = (140-age) × weight / (72 × SCr) × (0.85 if female)
Glomerulonephritis (proliferative disease). Pathognomonic for glomerular bleeding
Tubulointerstitial nephritis (pyelonephritis, drug-induced interstitial nephritis)
Acute tubular necrosis (ATN)
Non-specific, can be normal; seen with dehydration, fever, vigorous exercise
Benign condition in young people; protein in urine when upright but not when supine. Normal 24-hour urine protein when supine sample collected
X-linked hereditary nephritis (COL4A5 mutation — type IV collagen). Microscopic hematuria, progressive renal failure, sensorineural hearing loss, anterior lenticonus

cards.front: Thin basement membrane disease (benign familial hematuria)
cards.back: Autosomal dominant COL4A3/4 mutation. Persistent microscopic hematuria, normal renal function, excellent prognosis
Pre/post-procedure IV isotonic saline hydration. NAC (N-acetylcysteine) controversial. Minimize contrast volume. Avoid NSAIDs/nephrotoxins
Acidosis (severe metabolic), Electrolytes (hyperkalemia refractory to treatment), Ingestions (toxins), Overload (volume), Uremia (encephalopathy, pericarditis)

cards.front: Urine osmolality in SIADH vs diabetes insipidus
cards.back: SIADH: inappropriately concentrated urine (> 100 mOsm/kg despite low serum osmolality). DI: dilute urine (< 300 mOsm/kg despite hypernatremia)
